When glucose binds serum proteins, especially albumin, the product is called fructosamine.  When glucose concentrations are elevated persistently in blood as in diabetes mellitus, there is glycation of serum proteins, which results in increased fructosamine levels.
Fructosamine level reflects the average blood glucose levels in the past two to three weeks.
Fructosamine levels may below in severe hypoproteinemia. However, values appear unaffected when serum protein fluctuates within reference intervals.
